output:
**(B)** — All four created a separation between the measured interface and the actual system.

This is the precise structural pattern. Each case engineered a **proxy layer** that performed well under observation while the underlying reality operated differently:

- **Theranos** — the demo (measured interface) showed valid results from commercial machines; the actual system (miniaturized devices) couldn't produce them.
- **Enron** — the balance sheet (measured interface) showed healthy financials; the actual system (SPEs) held the real debt elsewhere.
- **VW** — the emissions test (measured interface) triggered clean mode; the actual system (normal driving) emitted up to 40x the legal limit.
- **WeWork** — the adjusted metric (measured interface) excluded real costs; the actual system (operations) was burning cash.

Why the other options fail:

- **(A)** is technically true but names the legal conclusion, not the structural pattern. It's like saying "all four broke the law" — correct but non-explanatory.
- **(C)** is an attribute of some cases but not a structural mechanism. It doesn't explain *how* the deception worked.
- **(D)** implies accidental failure. All four deliberately constructed the measurement/reality gap.

The deeper insight: this pattern — **Goodhart's Law weaponized** — works because stakeholders (investors, regulators, customers) can only observe the measurement layer. When you optimize the interface independently of the system, you can pass inspection indefinitely until the gap is externally exposed.
